Title: Neofunctionalization of an OMT cluster dominates polymethoxyflavone biosynthesis associated with the domestication of citrus
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Transient overexpression in citrus flavedo used pBI121-based constructs (constitutive CaMV 35S promoter, inferred from pBI121); RNAi used pK7GWIWG2D(II)-based constructs; stable callus overexpression promoter not explicitly stated
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Citrus reticulata and related Citrus spp.
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Red tangerine; Clementine; Guoqing No.1 satsuma mandarin; Ota Ponkan mandarin; Mangshan mandarin; multiple citrus accessions
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as an evolutionary and functional reference gene rather than a primary engineering target. OMT6 likely represents the ancestral copy from which the functional PMF OMT cluster evolved. Engineering may use OMT6 as a template for protein redesign; introducing the D17N-type neofunctionalizing change and/or stacking with OMT3/4/5 could help reconstruct PMF biosynthesis in low-PMF citrus or heterologous systems. Caution: native OMT6 generally shows weak or absent multisite PMF-forming activity and low expression in non-mandarin citrus.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes little to PMF variation in most citrus; some OMT6 orthologs show limited O-methylation of hydroxyflavones, while ancestral-like AbOMT6 has broader activity but is poorly expressed in primitive citrus. Functional deficiency of OMT6 relative to OMT4/5 helps explain lack of robust PMF accumulation outside mandarin-derived citrus.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics and synteny; cloning of OMT6 homologs from nine citrus accessions; recombinant protein assays on hydroxyflavone substrates; expression–metabolite correlation analysis; site-directed mutagenesis comparing CreOMT6 with CreOMT4.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit flavedo and developmental stage strongly affect PMF accumulation; comparisons included wild vs cultivated mandarins and diverse citrus taxa. No special abiotic treatment was central.
Common_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: No direct epigenetic regulation reported. Promoter structural variation and MITE insertions were analyzed, but the key demonstrated promoter effect was for OMT4 rather than OMT6.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In Red tangerine, expression of CreOMT6 correlated with total PMF content across fruit development, but overall OMT6 expression was negligible in citrus-related and early-diverging groups lacking the OMT cluster; lower than OMT3/4/5 significance for PMF variation.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Broadly same as applied species because genes are endogenous citrus genes; ancestral-like AbOMT6 from Atalantia buxfoliata was not expressed despite catalytic capacity.
Common_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Not reported explicitly; OMT6 ortholog IDs are species-specific genome annotations such as CreOMT6, CrmOMT6, AbOMT6, CmsOMT6
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: No experimentally validated protein partners reported
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: BSA/pool resequencing: BioProject PRJNA938971; RNA-seq: PRJNA341533, PRJNA579049, PRJNA776912; metabolomics/HPLC profiling across citrus germplasm
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: OMT6 is physically ~1.6 Mb downstream of the OMT cluster in C. reticulata. AbOMT6 can methylate multiple positions (C5, C6, C7, C3', C4'), but CreOMT4/5 gained C3 methylation not seen in AbOMT6. Comparative analysis supports duplication of ancestral OMT6 followed by neofunctionalization to form the mandarin OMT cluster.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Not directly tested. Likely tradeoff is lower PMFs in cultivated citrus due to domestication-associated selection rather than known growth penalties.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Plant flavonoid O-methyltransferase; PF08100-containing OMT family
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Citrus reticulata; Atalantia buxfoliata; other Citrus spp.
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Red tangerine; Mangshan mandarin; Clementine; primitive citrus accessions
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OMT6 is an ancestral homolog closely related to the tandem PMF OMT cluster. 2. Most OMT6 proteins are weak or deficient for the multisite methylation pattern needed for strong PMF biosynthesis, whereas AbOMT6 retains broader but insufficiently deployed activity. 3. A D17N substitution can convert CreOMT6 toward CreOMT4-like multisite activity, supporting neofunctionalization after duplication.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Polymethoxyflavones (PMFs), including nobiletin, tangeretin, sinensetin and related PMFs
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Fruit flavedo primarily; also leaves and flowers in citrus
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Anticancer and anti-inflammatory activities; protection against UV and pathogens; potential cardiovascular and metabolic health benefits

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Transient overexpression in citrus flavedo used pBI121-based constitutive expression (inferred CaMV 35S); stable citrus callus overexpression promoter not explicitly stated; RNAi used pK7GWIWG2D(II)-based silencing constructs
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Citrus reticulata and related Citrus spp.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Red tangerine; Guoqing No.1 satsuma mandarin; Ota Ponkan mandarin; Clementine; Mangshan mandarin
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Polymethoxyflavone biosynthesis within the flavone branch of flavonoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong engineering target for raising citrus PMF content. Best deployed by stacking with CreOMT4 and CreOMT5 to achieve complementary multisite methylation and fully methylated PMFs; can also be used in heterologous microbial reconstruction of the PMF pathway. Marker-assisted selection may combine high-expression alleles with favorable OMT4 promoter haplotypes. Caution: single-gene overexpression gives moderate increases and pathway precursor hydroxylation steps remain limiting.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group from S-adenosylmethionine to hydroxyl groups on hydroxyflavones, with strong activity especially at C6 and C4' and some activity at C3', thereby converting partially hydroxylated/methoxylated flavones into more highly methoxylated PMFs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes accumulation of multiple PMFs and contributes to total PMF content in citrus peel; overexpression increases PMFs, while RNAi decreases them.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: BSA mapping; transcript–metabolite correlation; yeast feeding assays with 28 substrates; purified recombinant enzyme kinetics; stable overexpression in citrus callus; transient overexpression and RNAi in citrus flavedo; metabolite profiling by HPLC/MS.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Terminal modification step directly forming target PMFs and converting intermediate hydroxy/partially methoxylated flavones toward final PMFs
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: PMF accumulation assayed mainly in fruit flavedo and leaves; developmental stage and taxonomic background affect interpretation.
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: No direct epigenetic mechanism reported for CreOMT3.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in PMF-rich Red tangerine flavedo; higher in wild and early-admixture mandarins than in modern cultivated mandarins; low/undetectable in PMF-poor tissues such as juice sacs and in non-mandarin citrus taxa.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species because endogenous citrus gene.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Not explicitly reported; gene symbol CreOMT3 (orthologs include CrmOMT3 etc.)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: No direct protein partners validated; functionally complements CreOMT4; CreOMT5
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Pool-seq/BSA: PRJNA938971; RNA-seq: PRJNA341533, PRJNA579049, PRJNA776912; metabolomics/HPLC profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CreOMT3 alone cannot fully methylate all hydroxyls on some substrates, but in combination with CreOMT4 and CreOMT5 yields diverse fully methylated PMFs. CreOMT3 is under purifying selection (median Ka/Ks < 1), unlike OMT4/5.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Flavone O-methylation branch leading to citrus PMFs
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No direct growth or yield penalties reported. Flux may still be limited by upstream hydroxylation and substrate supply.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: More highly methylated flavones including PMF intermediates and end products such as nobiletin-, tangeretin-, sinensetin-related products depending on substrate
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Hydroxyflavones and partially methoxylated flavones such as DMF1, 3'-demethylnobiletin, genkwanin, cirsimaritin
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a major terminal determinant of PMF variation, but not uniquely rate-limiting alone
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CreOMT3 was mapped to a major PMF locus and its expression strongly correlates with total PMFs. 2. It methylates hydroxyflavones mainly at C6 and C4' and contributes to six-site methylation capacity across the OMT cluster. 3. Overexpression raises, and RNAi lowers, PMF accumulation in citrus flavedo.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Human health-promoting metabolites with anticancer and anti-inflammatory properties; plant defense against UV and pathogens

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Transient overexpression in citrus flavedo used pBI121-based constitutive expression (inferred CaMV 35S); dual-luciferase assays used native promoter fragments; RNAi used pK7GWIWG2D(II)-based silencing constructs
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Citrus reticulata and related Citrus spp.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Red tangerine; Guoqing No.1 satsuma mandarin; Ota Ponkan mandarin; Clementine; Mangshan mandarin
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Polymethoxyflavone biosynthesis within the flavone branch of flavonoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Premier target for breeding and metabolic engineering of high-PMF citrus. Favor long-promoter allele containing the 1,041-bp MITE-containing fragment for marker-assisted selection; stack with CreOMT3 and CreOMT5 for maximal multisite methylation; D17/N17 residue knowledge enables protein engineering. Caution: domesticated mandarins often carry promoter deletion reducing expression; single-gene manipulation may alter intermediate/product balance such as 5-DNOB depletion due to conversion to nobiletin.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: SAM-dependent O-methyltransferase that methylates multiple positions on hydroxyflavones, notably C3, C5, C6, C7, and C3' depending on substrate, converting hydroxyflavones and PMF intermediates toward final PMFs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: A crucial determinant of PMF biosynthesis; promotes accumulation of major PMFs including nobiletin and sinensetin. Overexpression increases total PMFs, while RNAi lowers them. Promoter deletion in cultivated mandarins reduces expression and PMF content.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: BSA mapping; transcript–metabolite correlation; yeast substrate feeding; purified enzyme kinetic assays; transient overexpression and RNAi in citrus flavedo; stable callus overexpression; site-directed mutagenesis (N17D/T168A); dual-luciferase promoter assays; germplasm genotyping.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Direct terminal product-forming and intermediate-converting step in PMF biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit flavedo developmental stage and citrus domestication status are central; promoter haplotype differs between wild/early-admixture and modern cultivated mandarins.
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: No classic epigenetic mechanism shown. Cis-regulatory structural variation in the promoter is key: a 1,041-bp fragment containing two MITEs markedly elevates promoter activity and associates with high PMF accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in PMF-rich flavedo of Red tangerine and in wild/early-admixture mandarins; lower in modern cultivated mandarins; low in PMF-poor tissues and taxa.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species because endogenous citrus gene.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Not explicitly reported; gene symbol CreOMT4 (orthologs include CrmOMT4, CrpOMT4)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: No direct physical partners validated; functionally complements CreOMT3; CreOMT5
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Pool-seq/BSA: PRJNA938971; RNA-seq: PRJNA341533, PRJNA579049, PRJNA776912; metabolomics/HPLC profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: N17 is essential for C3-, C5-, C6- and C3'-O-methylation activity; CreOMT4-N17D loses most activity except residual C7 methylation. T168A enhances 5-O-methylation but moderately reduces C3 methylation. Long promoter allele gives ~3-fold higher LUC activity than short cultivated allele.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Flavone O-methylation branch leading to citrus PMFs
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can reduce intermediate 5-demethylnobiletin by further conversion to nobiletin; no growth penalty reported.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Highly methylated PMFs including nobiletin and related products depending on substrate; also methylated intermediates from DMF1, LYS, 3'DNOB, GEN, CIR
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Hydroxyflavones and partially methylated PMFs such as 3-OH-flavone, gardenin B, DMF1, lysionotin, 3'-demethylnobiletin, genkwanin, cirsimaritin
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Established as a key control/major determinant of PMF variation in citrus, especially through expression polymorphism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CreOMT4 is a major PMF biosynthetic gene identified by genetics, expression, and enzymology. 2. It performs multisite O-methylation and is especially important for converting intermediates toward major PMFs. 3. A 1,041-bp MITE-containing promoter fragment boosts expression, and its deletion in cultivated mandarins is associated with reduced PMF accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Polymethoxyflavones (PMFs), including nobiletin, tangeretin, sinensetin, 5-demethylnobiletin-derived products
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Anticancer, anti-inflammatory, cardiovascular and metabolic health-related functions; plant protection from UV and pathogens

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Transient overexpression in citrus flavedo used pBI121-based constitutive expression (inferred CaMV 35S); stable callus overexpression promoter not explicitly stated; RNAi used pK7GWIWG2D(II)-based silencing constructs
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Citrus reticulata and related Citrus spp.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Red tangerine; Guoqing No.1 satsuma mandarin; Ota Ponkan mandarin; Clementine; Mangshan mandarin
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Polymethoxyflavone biosynthesis within the flavone branch of flavonoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for PMF biofortification. Best used in multigene stacking with CreOMT3 and CreOMT4 because it complements regioselectivity and strongly methylates C3' and C5/C7 positions. Applicable to citrus improvement and microbial PMF production. Caution: may shift pools away from intermediates such as 5-DNOB toward more methylated end products.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: SAM-dependent O-methyltransferase with strong activity at C7, C5, and C3' and capacity for C3/C6-related transformations on certain substrates, converting hydroxyflavones and PMF intermediates to more highly methylated PMFs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Crucial for accumulation of multiple PMFs and total PMF content; overexpression elevates PMFs in flavedo while RNAi reduces them.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: BSA mapping; transcript–metabolite correlation; yeast feeding assays; purified recombinant enzyme kinetics; stable callus overexpression; transient overexpression and RNAi in citrus flavedo; metabolite profiling.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Direct terminal modification step forming target PMFs and converting intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit flavedo and leaf PMF-rich tissues; domestication status and tissue specificity affect expression and impact.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in PMF-rich Red tangerine flavedo; higher in wild and early-admixture mandarins than in modern cultivated mandarins; low in PMF-poor tissues and taxa.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species because endogenous citrus gene.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Not explicitly reported; gene symbol CreOMT5 (orthologs include CrmOMT5 etc.)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: No direct physical partners validated; functionally complements CreOMT3; CreOMT4
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Pool-seq/BSA: PRJNA938971; RNA-seq: PRJNA341533, PRJNA579049, PRJNA776912; metabolomics/HPLC profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CreOMT5 cannot methylate genkwanin in vitro but shows high efficiency on DMF1, lysionotin and 3'DNOB. Like CreOMT4, it gained C3-O-methylation capacity relative to ancestral OMT6, supporting neofunctionalization in mandarin-derived citrus.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Flavone O-methylation branch leading to citrus PMFs
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can decrease intermediate 5-demethylnobiletin by converting it onward to nobiletin; no plant growth penalties reported.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Highly methylated PMFs including nobiletin-related and sinensetin/tangeretin-related products depending on substrate
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Hydroxyflavones and partially methylated PMF intermediates such as 3-OH-flavone, gardenin B, DMF1, lysionotin, 3'-demethylnobiletin, cirsimaritin
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: A major terminal determinant and key contributor, especially in combination with OMT3/4
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CreOMT5 is one of three tandemly duplicated OMTs underlying PMF biosynthesis in citrus. 2. It efficiently methylates several hydroxyflavones, especially at C5, C7 and C3', and collaborates with OMT3/4 to produce fully methylated PMFs. 3. Overexpression increases, and RNAi suppression decreases, PMF accumulation in citrus flavedo.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Health-promoting bioactive flavones with anticancer/anti-inflammatory roles and plant protective functions
